region_of_origin: China (adapted widely across South Asia and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specifically for hot and sour soup; closest parent 'soup food product' (FOODON_00002572) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Soups and broths) is the closest parent category; no specific hot and sour soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for hot and sour soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of hot and sour soup is approximately 300 g, typically served as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and Sour Soup, restaurant-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for hot and sour soup (restaurant-style), cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN data for Indo-Chinese soup preparations. The dish is served in both vegetarian and non-vegetarian variants; nutrient values represent a composite mid-point estimate. Non-vegetarian variant typically adds chicken or egg, slightly increasing protein. Values are per 100 g of prepared soup.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed soups and broth-based dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable-based soups and starch-thickened broths
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot and sour soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous starch-thickened vegetable soups (GI range 25–45 per Sydney GI Database). The cornstarch thickener contributes moderate glycemic load but the overall carbohydrate content per 100 g is low (~4.5 g), yielding a low GI category. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 4.5 × 3) / 100 = 4.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit hot and sour soup intake or request a reduced-sodium preparation, as a single 300 g bowl can contribute approximately 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot and sour soup has a low glycemic index and a low glycemic load per serving (~4.7), making it a generally suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar and with attention to portion size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat; low overall fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content of hot and sour soup poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease; however, the low total fat and low saturated fat content are favourable, and occasional consumption in moderate portions is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and Sour Soup (酸辣汤, Suān là tāng) is a popular broth-based soup originating from Chinese cuisine and widely adapted across South Asia and Southeast Asia as an Indo-Chinese dish. It is characterised by a sharp sour flavour from vinegar and a spicy heat from white pepper and chilli, balanced with a savoury umami base from soy sauce and stock. The soup is typically thickened with cornstarch to give it a silky, slightly viscous texture. Common ingredients include tofu, wood ear mushrooms, bamboo shoots, egg ribbons, and spring onions, with the non-vegetarian variant incorporating shredded chicken or prawn. In Singapore and Indian restaurant contexts, it is frequently offered as a starter in both vegetarian and non-vegetarian versions. Nutritionally, hot and sour soup is relatively low in calories (~35 kcal per 100 g) and fat (~1 g per 100 g), with a moderate protein content (~2.1 g per 100 g). Its primary clinical concern is sodium, with a standard 300 g bowl delivering approximately 960 mg of sodium, which is significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. The dish is dairy-free and can be prepared halal. It is commonly served as a first course in North Indian and Indo-Chinese restaurant menus across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this specific dish as served at this restaurant. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite data for restaurant-style hot and sour soup, cross-referenced with Indo-Chinese soup preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and the dual veg/non-veg nature of the dish.
notes: 1. This record covers both vegetarian and non-vegetarian variants of hot and sour soup as served at Bombay Curry And Kebabs North Indian Cuisine, South Bridge Road, Singapore (field survey listing). The non-vegetarian variant typically contains shredded chicken or egg, which slightly increases protein and cholesterol values. Nutrient values represent a composite mid-point estimate applicable to both variants. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for this soup category. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (35) based on starch-thickened vegetable soups from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for hot and sour soup. 5. The dish is classified as Indo-Chinese / Pan-Asian given its Chinese origin and widespread adaptation in Indian and Singaporean restaurant contexts. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish convention (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the Singapore restaurant context. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA composite data and should be treated as approximate.
